cards.text: The {{c1::noexcept}} specifier is placed after the function parameter list and indicates the function {{c2::will not throw}} any exceptions.

cards.question: How do you declare a function that won't throw exceptions?
cards.answer: Add the noexcept specifier after the parameter list.
cards.explanation: The noexcept specifier tells the compiler and users that this function won't throw exceptions.

cards.front: What happens if a noexcept function throws an exception?
cards.back: std::terminate() is called immediately, ending the program. Stack unwinding may or may not occur.

cards.text: The noexcept specifier can take a {{c1::boolean expression}}: noexcept(true) means no throw, noexcept(false) means {{c2::may throw}}. Plain noexcept is equivalent to noexcept({{c3::true}}).

cards.question: What does this template noexcept specification mean?
cards.answer: The function is noexcept only if T's move constructor is noexcept.
cards.explanation: Conditional noexcept allows a function's exception guarantee to depend on template parameters or other compile-time conditions.

cards.text: The {{c1::noexcept}} operator is a compile-time operator that returns {{c2::true}} if an expression is declared noexcept, otherwise returns {{c3::false}}.

cards.question: What does noexcept(noexcept(expr)) do?
cards.answer: The function is noexcept if t.someMethod() is noexcept. The outer noexcept is the specifier, the inner is the operator.
cards.explanation: noexcept(noexcept(expr)) checks if expr is noexcept and uses that result as the noexcept specification.

cards.front: What functions should be marked noexcept?
cards.back:
Functions that:
  • Are guaranteed not to throw (simple getters)
  • Destructors (implicitly noexcept)
  • Move constructors and move assignment
  • Swap functions
  • Performance-critical functions where no-throw enables optimizations

cards.text: Destructors are {{c1::implicitly noexcept}} by default. You should never explicitly mark them noexcept({{c2::false}}) because throwing from destructors during unwinding causes {{c3::std::terminate()}}.

cards.explanation:
noexcept allows the compiler to optimize because:
  • No exception handling code needed
  • Move operations can be used instead of copy (vector reallocation)
  • Smaller binary size (no exception tables)
  • Better inlining opportunities
  • Enables certain STL optimizations

cards.front: What replaced dynamic exception specifications (throw()) in C++11?
cards.back: The noexcept specifier replaced throw(). Dynamic exception specifications were deprecated in C++11 and removed in C++17.

cards.question: Why should move operations be noexcept?
cards.answer: STL containers use move operations only if they're noexcept, otherwise they copy for exception safety. Marking them noexcept enables performance optimizations.
cards.explanation: STL containers like vector need strong exception guarantee during reallocation. They only use move if it's noexcept, otherwise copy.
